html:
Hedvig Charlotta Nordenflycht, born November 28, 1718, in Stockholm, and died June 29, 1763, in the parish of Skoklosters, was a Swedish author. She was the daughter of Anders Andersson Nordbohm (ennobled under the name Nordenflycht), who served as a chamberlain at the Chamber Collegium, and Christina Rosin. She is sometimes considered to be Sweden's first feminist and is regarded as one of the foremost Swedish poets of the 18th century. She is also unique in that she was one of the first Swedish women to support herself through her writing.

**Upbringing and Marriage**

Nordenflycht grew up in Stockholm, but in 1731 moved to the family's estate, Viby, in the parish of Harbo, Uppsala County. After a promise made on her father's deathbed in 1734, she became engaged to her teacher, Johan Tideman, who died in 1737 before the marriage could take place. Nordenflycht was greatly influenced by Tideman's sharp intellect, but he was an advocate of the Enlightenment-era rationalism and skepticism that Nordenflycht would later reject. [citation needed] Shortly after Tideman's death, she met the priest Jacob Fabricius and married him in 1741. Their marriage proved to be short but also happy. Her husband fell ill and died only nine months after their wedding. As a result of this, Nordenflycht moved from Karlskrona, where the couple had relocated when Fabricius was appointed as a naval chaplain, to a small cottage on Lidingö. Her life was marked by grief, and she described her home with the words: "The entire room, adorned with sad paintings, testified to my longing, and a cymbal with a few birds were the only signs of joy..."

**Literary Works**

Nordenflycht gained fame with her collection of poems, "Den sörgande turtur-dufwan" (1743), written in mourning after the death of her husband. From 1744 to 1750, she published four volumes titled "Qwinligit Tankespel." She closely followed the debates of her time, and many of her works are characterized as opinion pieces on political, philosophical, and religious issues, as well as on women's freedom and rights. In addition to poetry, Nordenflycht also wrote rococo poems (such as "Dorillas kväde"). Among her works, "Fruentimrets försvar" (1761) is particularly noteworthy, in which Nordenflycht defends the female gender against Rousseau's criticism of women's role in the theater. Another important work is "Fruentimbers Plikt at upöfwa deras Wett, 1741," where she criticizes the oppression of women and advocates for women's right to education.

Nordenflycht's most widely read poem, "Öfver en Hyacint" (1762), is a declaration of love to a younger man named Johan Fischerström, a physiocrat and economist, who became her last love. The later poems deal with the romantic drama between these three individuals: Nordenflycht, Fischerström, and Countess Cathérine Charlotte De la Gardie, which unfolded during the winter of 1762–63. According to accounts of her death, "...abandoned by him whose affection she believed she possessed, she threw herself into the stream flowing past her home. She was rescued alive but died three days later, on June 28, 1763," at the age of 44.

=== Nordenflycht as a Female Author ===

Nordenflycht was a female author who wrote for women. This was unusual in the 18th century, even in Sweden. Nordenflycht surpassed all the limitations placed on women during her time and assumed a "male" role simply by being an author visible in the public sphere. A woman's position in the 18th century varied depending on class, marital status, and age. Because Nordenflycht was upper-class, she had more opportunities for education and financial resources. Female authors during this period often had to rely on men in their lives to support their writing and allow them to create. Nordenflycht was also a widow, which gave her the right to manage her own finances. This meant she had greater opportunities to publish her works and live independently compared to many other women.

While female authors existed, they typically wrote occasional poems and rarely achieved public recognition. Nordenflycht did write occasional poems, like other women. In fact, over half of her writings were occasional poems. This was because, during that time, occasional poetry was considered "lesser" literature and was a type of writing that women were "allowed" to produce publicly. Nordenflycht was unique in that, as a woman, she not only wrote occasional poems but also successfully published her beliefs about the intellectual capabilities of women and advocated for women's rights through her writing. Nordenflycht was the only Swedish female author during this period who, besides occasional poetry, also wrote in "high literary" genres (verse epics, drama, and love poetry). Furthermore, Nordenflycht was the most prolific writer on the topic of women's issues. Nordenflycht did not shy away from expressing her hope that she could use her poetic talent for something more, and...

How certain can we be about this?

The individuals from the Age of Liberty are well-documented through parliamentary records, letters, and archives. However, interpretations of their roles are often influenced by later political biases.
tldr: Hedvig Charlotta Nordenflycht was a well-known Swedish author in the 18th century who advocated for women's rights and education.
lattlast:
Hedvig Charlotta Nordenflycht was born in Stockholm in 1718. She became known as one of the most prominent poets of her time and is often considered Sweden's first feminist. What made her unique was that she managed to earn money from her writing, which was very unusual for women in the 18th century.

Hedvig lived a life marked by both success and sorrow. After her first fiancé died before they could marry, she married a priest who passed away just nine months later. Despite this profound grief, she continued to write extensively about important topics such as politics, religion, and women's right to education.

She was remarkable because she not only wrote short poems for celebrations but also addressed difficult issues in public debates. Through her books, she defended women's ability to think critically and demanded that they be granted the same rights as men. She died in 1963 at the age of 44 following a tragic love affair.
